name: Zufre Reservoir Loop
name_original: Desde Zufre al Castillo de las Guardas y Embalse de Zufre – circular por Higuera de la Sierra
description: This difficult 40.9-mile touring cycling route from Zufre offers challenging climbs and scenic views of the Embalse de Zufre and Andalusian
region_intro: Touring cycling around Zufre is characterized by its location within the Sierra de Aracena and Picos de Aroche Natural Park in Huelva, Spain. The region features rolling hills covered in holm oak and cork oak groves, interspersed with olive groves and pastures. Numerous streams crisscross the landscape, and the prominent Zufre Reservoir offers expansive views. The terrain consists of gentle mountains and undulating hills, with the town itself perched at an elevation of 1,463 feet (446 meters). Best touring cycling routes around Zufre The most popular touring cycling route is Zufre Reservoir Loop, a 40.9 miles (65.9 km) trail that takes 4 hours 35 minutes to complete. This route offers views of the reservoir and the surrounding natural park. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is La Minilla Reservoir – La Cantina loop from El Ronquillo, a difficult 42.5 miles (68.5 km) path. This route traverses varied terrain including rolling hills and oak groves. Local touring cyclists also love the Vereda de Sevilla loop from Higuera de la Sierra, a 28.4 miles (45.6 km) trail leading through the natural park, often completed in about 3 hours 58 minutes. Touring cycling around Zufre is defined by rolling hills, holm oak and cork oak groves, and the Zufre Reservoir. The network offers options for different ability levels, including paths with minimal incline. The routes in Zufre are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 400 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Zufre's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Zufre?
A: There are over 50 touring cycling routes around Zufre, offering a variety of options for exploring the Sierra de Aracena and Picos de Aroche Natural Park.

Q: What is the terrain like for touring cycling in Zufre?
A: The terrain around Zufre is characterized by rolling hills and gentle mountains, with numerous holm oak and cork oak groves. While many routes involve undulating hills, there are also paths with minimal incline, such as the 'Carril Blanco' which connects Zufre with Higuera de la Sierra.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while cycling in Zufre?
A: Many routes offer stunning views of the &lt;a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zufre" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zufre Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, the largest in the Rivera de Huelva basin. You can also encounter diverse landscapes within the natural park. Notable nearby attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5300354" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Grotto of the Marvels&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4402997" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Río Tinto&lt;/a&gt;, and various viewpoints like the Paseo de los Alcaldes, offering panoramic vistas.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural sites to explore near the cycling routes?
A: Yes, Zufre's town center is a Site of Cultural Interest, featuring narrow streets reflecting its Islamic past. You can visit the 16th-century Town Hall, the Church of the Immaculate Conception, and a historic bullring. The 'Water Route' highlights ancient fountains and public washing places throughout the municipality, some dating back to the 16th century.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Zufre?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in Zufre are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e986883432/zufre-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zufre Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular circular route offering extensive views of the reservoir and surrounding natural park.

Q: Are there challenging touring cycling routes in Zufre?
A: Yes, the majority of touring cycling routes in Zufre are rated as difficult, featuring significant elevation gains and varied terrain. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20126294" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Minilla Reservoir – La Cantina loop from El Ronquillo&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 42.5 miles (68.5 km) with nearly 1,000 meters of ascent.

Q: Are there family-friendly touring cycling routes in Zufre?
A: While many routes are rated difficult, the region does offer paths with minimal incline suitable for a more relaxed pace. The 'Carril Blanco' is explicitly mentioned as a trail suitable for bicycles with minimal incline, providing a scenic route through the natural park. Always check the route details for specific difficulty and elevation before heading out with family.

Q: What is the best time to visit Zufre for touring cycling?
A: Zufre's location within the Sierra de Aracena and Picos de Aroche Natural Park makes it enjoyable for cycling during most seasons. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for touring cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and potential chill of winter.

Q: Is there parking available for touring cyclists in Zufre?
A: Zufre is a small town, and parking is generally available within or near the town center. For specific routes starting from nearby villages like Higuera de la Sierra or El Ronquillo, parking options would be available in those locations.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy about the routes in Zufre?
A: The touring cycling routes in Zufre are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the stunning natural beauty of the Sierra de Aracena and Picos de Aroche Natural Park, the expansive views of the Zufre Reservoir, and the diverse terrain that offers both challenging climbs and scenic, gentler paths.

Q: Where can I find refreshments or accommodation near the cycling routes in Zufre?
A: Zufre itself offers local cafes, restaurants, and some accommodation options. As you cycle through the natural park, smaller villages and towns like Higuera de la Sierra or El Ronquillo, which are starting points for some routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31512781" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vereda de Sevilla loop from Higuera de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt;, also provide places for refreshments and rest.

Q: Can I bring my dog on touring cycling routes in Zufre?
A: While the natural park is generally welcoming to dogs, it's important to keep them on a leash, especially in protected areas or near livestock. Always check specific trail regulations or local signage, as some areas might have restrictions for wildlife protection.
tips: From this viewpoint at the bottom of the park you can see the Zufre reservoir.
Next to the mountain pass called Puerto de las Eras, this 18th century hermitage is located and a recreational area with benches and tables where you can rest for a while.
Views of the capricious shapes of the Zufre Reservoir - a little to the east of this point there is a path to the dam.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This challenging touring bicycle route is approximately 65.9 kilometers long and has an estimated duration of around 4 hours and 35 minutes of pure cycling time. However, plan for a longer day to account for breaks, enjoying the views, and navigating the difficult terrain.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is rated as difficult and requires an excellent physical condition due to its significant elevation gain of over 1,000 meters and its length. It is not recommended for beginners.

faqs.question: Where does the route start and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: As a circular route, you can technically start at any point. However, common starting points would be in Zufre or Higuera de la Sierra. Parking availability will vary by town; look for public parking areas within these villages.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route features a mix of surfaces. Approximately 51.0 km are paved asphalt roads, and 8.61 km are tarmac. There are also about 6.09 km of unpaved sections, which could include dirt or gravel paths. This varied terrain requires a touring bicycle capable of handling diverse surfaces, such as a hybrid or gravel bike.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: While cycling, you'll pass by several notable spots. Keep an eye out for the Viewpoint of Zufre, offering great vistas. You'll also encounter the Hermitage of the Virgin of the Port, and the route provides views of the Zufre Reservoir.

faqs.question: Can I swim in Embalse de Zufre?
faqs.answer: While the route offers views of the Zufre Reservoir, official and safe swimming spots are not explicitly highlighted along this specific cycling path. Always check local regulations and safety guidelines before attempting to swim in natural bodies of water.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The region experiences hot summers. Spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for cycling, avoiding the extreme heat of July and August. Winter can also be suitable, though days are shorter and temperatures cooler.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This is a cycling route primarily on roads and mixed terrain. While there are no specific prohibitions against dogs on public roads, it's generally not advisable to bring dogs on long, difficult cycling tours, especially given the varied surfaces and potential for traffic. If you do, ensure they are well-trained and can keep pace safely.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route or the Parque Natural Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route or to access the general areas of the Parque Natural Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche through which it passes. However, always respect local regulations and private property.

faqs.question: Does the route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route intersect with or run alongside other paths. You'll find it briefly crosses parts of the "Antigua linea ferrea Peña de Hierro - (Ronquillo) - San Juan de Aznalfarache", "Camino de Acceso a Carril Blanco", "Sendero Fuente Mimbrera", "Las Tobas", and "Vereda de Sevilla".

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes through towns like Zufre, Castillo de las Guardas, and Higuera de la Sierra. These villages typically offer local cafes, bars, and restaurants where you can refuel and find refreshments. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer, more remote sections.

waypoints.name: Hermitage of the Virgin of the Port
